coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot modified electrospun TiO2 nanofibers for flexible formaldehyde gas sensor under UV illumination at room temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qingyu
Fan, Shuxing
Fu, Lijuan
Liu, Chaoqiang
Xu, Jiapeng
Tang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A flexible gas sensor based on carbon quantum dot decorated TiO2 nanofibers (CQD/TiO2) was prepared by hydrothermal and electrostatic spinning methods to detect formaldehyde at room temperature. SEM, TEM, EDS, XRD and XPS were used to analyze the morphology, structure and composition of the composite. The gas sensing test results show that the working temperature of the material is reduced to room temperature by UV excitation, and subsequently, CQD/TiO2 exhibits a lower detection limit and faster response/recovery time for formaldehyde by carbon material modification. The flexibility test of CQD/TiO2 shows that the composite material of metal oxide and carbon material has a certain degree of flexibility. The enhanced sensing performance by UV excitation and CQD modification is attributed mainly to the photogenerated electron transfer and the formation of p-n heterojunctions. The modification of TiO2 with carbon quantum dots provides an experimental reference for the development of room-temperature flexible MOS-based formaldehyde sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Formaldehyde
Gas sensor
Room temperature
Electrospinning

Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61801276 ), and Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation ( ZR2018BF003 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of (a) synthesis of CQD, (b) synthesis of TiO2 nanofibers, (c) synthesis of CQD/TiO2 and device preparation.
SEM images of (a) TiO2 nanofibers, (b) CQD/TiO2; (c) TEM image of CQD/TiO2; (d) HRTEM of CQD/TiO2.
(a-e) EDS elemental mapping images of CQD/TiO2; (f) EDS energy spectrum of CQD/TiO2.
XRD patterns of CQD/TiO2 and TiO2 nanofibers.
(a) XPS spectra of CQD/TiO2; The fine spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) Ti 2p, (d) O 1 s.

(a) Response curves of TiO2 to 30 ppm formaldehyde versus different operating temperatures; Response and recovery time of TiO2 to 10 ppm formaldehyde (b) under natural light at 275 °C, (c) under UV light at 25 °C; (d) Transient curves of TiO2 UV at 25 °C to 10–200 ppm formaldehyde.

(a) Selectivity of CQD/TiO2 to 10 ppm ammonia, formaldehyde, ethanol, acetone and methanol under UV light; (b) Response and recovery time of CQD/TiO2 under UV light; (c) Transient curves of CQD/TiO2 UV at 25 °C to 0.5–20 ppm formaldehyde.
(a) Flexible test model of CQD/TiO2; Change in resistance of the sensor (b) under different bending degrees, (c) under different bending times.
(a) Responses curves of CQD/TiO2 sensor to 10 ppm formaldehyde at different relative humidity; (b) Long-term stability of CQD/TiO2 sensor.
Schematic diagram of sensing mechanism of CQD/TiO2 (a) in air, (b) in formaldehyde. Mechanistic diagrams of CQD/TiO2 (c) in natural light, (d) in UV light;
Properties of gas sensors based on different materials in this work.
Properties of gas sensors based on different materials.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot modified electrospun TiO2 nanofibers for flexible formaldehyde gas sensor under UV illumination at room temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong 250358, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Electronics, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, Shandong 250358, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A flexible gas sensor based on carbon quantum dot decorated TiO2 nanofibers (CQD/TiO2) was prepared by hydrothermal and electrostatic spinning methods to detect formaldehyde at room temperature. SEM, TEM, EDS, XRD and XPS were used to analyze the morphology, structure and composition of the composite. The gas sensing test results show that the working temperature of the material is reduced to room temperature by UV excitation, and subsequently, CQD/TiO2 exhibits a lower detection limit and faster response/recovery time for formaldehyde by carbon material modification. The flexibility test of CQD/TiO2 shows that the composite material of metal oxide and carbon material has a certain degree of flexibility. The enhanced sensing performance by UV excitation and CQD modification is attributed mainly to the photogenerated electron transfer and the formation of p-n heterojunctions. The modification of TiO2 with carbon quantum dots provides an experimental reference for the development of room-temperature flexible MOS-based formaldehyde sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Formaldehyde (HCHO) is an important raw material and is widely used in chemical, biological and textile fields [1–6]. It is also used in the field of pharmacology and medicine due to its bactericidal effect [7–9]. Since formaldehyde was applied to glue production, formaldehyde has been widely used in house decoration, and it mainly exists in indoor building materials and furniture, such as paints, carpets, curtains, and wooden furniture [10–12]. However, formaldehyde is a highly toxic, widespread and volatile hazardous gas, which is the main cause of sick building syndrome (SBS) [13,14]. Some studies have shown that human contact with HCHO exhibits irritating effects on the skin and mucous membranes, and long-term exposure to formaldehyde can cause cardiovascular disease, respiratory failure, endocrine disorders, immune system derangement, and in severe cases, even cancer and death [15–18]. Therefore, formaldehyde has been classified as an indoor and outdoor air pollutant that poses a significant risk to human health [19]. Nowadays, among all formaldehyde detection methods, the traditional detection methods of spectrophotometry, electrochemistry and chromatography are expensive and complicated to operate. The gas sensor method will gradually become the dominant method because of its low production cost, easy mass production and high thermal stability.

Carbon quantum dot (CQD) is a nanomaterial consisting of a carbonaceous backbone, usually containing sp2 hybridized graphitic carbon or sp2-sp3 hybridized carbon mixed with amorphous carbon, and the particle size is <5 nm [20]. CQD shows fast photogenerated electron transfer ability, excellent upconversion luminescence and fluorescence properties and has been successfully used as an excellent electron acceptor and donor [21–24]. Based on different CQD precursors, materials can be obtained with different surface functional groups that can selectively react with certain gases. ZnO nanoflowers were physically compounded with CQD and the gas sensing test found a lower limit of detection of NO down to 100 ppb [25]. C. Marilena et al. [26] prepared CQD by citric acid pyrolysis, NiO by hydrothermal method, and then mixed CQD and NiO to prepare hybrid material which could detect 0.1 ppm methane at 150 °C.

TiO2 was first applied to photocatalysis by A. Fujishima et al. [27] in 1972, which laid the foundation for the research and application of TiO2. Since 1972 researchers have conducted a lot of research on TiO2, and now TiO2 is not only applied to photocatalysis, but also used in gas sensors, anti-corrosion, and optical coatings. The unique optical and electrical properties of TiO2 depend on its crystal structure. The crystal structure of TiO2 is mainly divided into brookite structure belonging to the orthorhombic system and anatase & rutile phase structure belonging to the tetragonal system, while the brookite structure is unstable, thus less application. Anatase and rutile have relatively wide application fields [28,29]. Previous studies have found that TiO2 is a UV-sensitive material and its electrochemical properties can be enhanced by UV excitation, and based on this finding UV-activated TiO2 based formaldehyde sensors have achieved excellent development [30–32].

In this study, TiO2 nanofibers were synthesized via electrostatic spinning, followed by mechanical milling and integration with carbon quantum dots to fabricate CQD/TiO2 composite sensors for the electrochemical detection of formaldehyde. Subsequently, the morphology, crystal structure, and composition of CQD/TiO2 were characterized and analyzed, and the gas sensing performance of the composites was scrutinized in detail. The results of the gas sensing experiments indicated that by stepwise UV excitation and CQD modification to pure TiO2, the operating temperature was reduced by 250 °C, the response and recovery time were significantly improved, and the detection limit of formaldehyde was reduced to 0.5 ppm. Additionally, the sensing mechanism of CQD/TiO2 composites was discussed by leveraging the photosensitive properties of CQD and TiO2.

l-ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), ethanol (C2H6O), glacial acetic acid (C2H4O2), tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, [C6H9NO]x) were analytical grade and without further purification.

As shown in Fig. 1(a), a bottom-up approach was used to prepare carbon quantum dots in this work. Firstly, 400 mg of l-ascorbic acid was weighed on a microbalance and added to a mixture of 4 mL of ethylene glycol and 6 mL of deionized water with magnetic stirring for 8 h to make them fully mixed. Secondly, the above solution was transferred to a 15 mL autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 70 min to obtain the carbon quantum dot dispersion. The 9 mL of carbon quantum dot dispersion contains about 1.5 g of carbon quantum dots. Finally, the dispersion was put into a sealed container for storage.

In this work, TiO2 nanofibers were prepared by electrostatic spinning with a single nozzle, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Firstly, 3.4 g of tetrabutyl titanate and 1 g of PVP were dissolved in 15 g of ethanol and 4 g of glacial acetic acid with magnetic stirring for 6 h to obtain the electrostatic spinning precursor solution. Secondly, the precursor solution was transferred to a 10 mL syringe. The relevant parameters were as follows: the distance and the voltage between the needle and collector were 4 cm and 22 kV respectively. The needle diameter was 0.5 mm. The propulsion speed of the built-in syringe pump was set to 0.1 mL/min. The spinning temperature was 30 °C, and the relative humidity was 45 % RH. After a certain time, the sample was collected on the aluminum foil receiver plate. Finally, the above collected sample was placed in a muffle furnace and calcined at a rate of 2 °C/min from room temperature to 500 °C for 2 h to obtain TiO2 nanofibers.

Firstly, 0.02 g of TiO2 nanofibers were placed in an agate grinding bowl, subsequently, 100 ul of CQD dispersion was dropped into the bowl and ground into a paste. Secondly, the paste was evenly applied to the PET flexible substrate with gold electrodes using a brush and shade dried for 12 h to evaporate the remaining moisture. Finally, the electrodes were placed in a drying oven at 60 °C for 48 h to completely decompose the moisture. The preparation process of the CQD modified TiO2 nanofibers is shown in Fig. 1(c).

The utilization of a Sigma 500 field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) was employed in observing the sample morphology. The microstructure and crystal lattice were scrutinized using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM) under an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray energy spectrometer (EDS) was utilized in the characterization of the elemental composition of the samples. X-ray diffractometer (XRD) data were obtained by Bruker D8 Advance, diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation and diffraction angle 2θ range of 5–90° in steps of 0.03°. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) data were obtained by Thermpfisher EscaLab 250Xi.

Firstly, the prepared sample powder was mixed with DI water and ground into a paste in an agate grinding bowl. And then the paste was applied to PET flexible substrate with a pair of gold electrodes (20 mm × 5 mm × 0.05 mm, electrode spacing 50 μm) using a paint pen. The substrates were dried at 60 °C for 48 h with a heating rate of 5 °C/min to completely remove DI water. At last, CGS-MT comprehensive gas sensing test platform was used to measure the gas sensing performance of the sensors. The target gases in the gas sensing tests were liquid-phase VOC gases, and the gas concentrations were calculated by EtLiquidVolume software. The underlying formula of the software is Q = (V × C × M) / (22.4 × d × ρ) × 10−9 × (273 + TR) / (273 + TB). Q (mL) is the liquid-phase gas injection volume, V (mL) is the test bottle volume, C (ppm) is the target gas concentration, M (g) is the molecular weight, d is the liquid purity, ρ (g/cm3) is the liquid density, TR (°C) is the environmental temperature, and TB (°C) is the test bottle internal temperature. The gas response value is denoted by S = Ra/Rg, where Ra and Rg represent the resistance of the sensors in the air and target gas, respectively. The response and recovery time are defined as the time required for a 90 % change in resistance. The humidity test is achieved by injecting a quantity of DI water into the test bottle.

The morphology of the prepared samples was characterized by SEM, and Fig. 2(a) shows the SEM image of TiO2 nanofibers, from which it can be seen that the surface of the fibers is smooth, the length diameter ratios are relatively large (about 80 nm in diameter), and the thickness are uniform without sticking. The SEM image of TiO2 nanofibers compounded with carbon quantum dots is shown in Fig. 2(b), and it is found that the long TiO2 fibers are destroyed and changed into short rods with a length of about 600 nm after grinding. Moreover, it is worth noting that the originally smooth TiO2 nanofiber surface is covered by many particles, which can be observed clearly in the TEM image of CQD/TiO2 in Fig. 2(c). The lattice stripe information was analyzed by HRTEM image (Fig. 2(d)), and the lattice spacing is 0.26 nm, which is in the range of carbon quantum dots lattice spacing (0.20– 0.35 nm) [33]. Fig. 3 shows the EDS characterization of CQD/TiO2, and the results show that the composite sample contains C, Ti and O elements, where the C element around the fiber is derived from carbon tape. Fig. 3(c-e) elemental mapping shows that C and Ti elements are distributed in the same fiber. Combined with the above analysis, the particles covered by the surface of TiO2 nanofibers are carbon quantum dots.

The XRD patterns of the ground and dried samples are shown in Fig. 4 . The diffraction peaks at 2θ of 25.4°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 55.1°, 62.8° and 75.1° are assigned to TiO2 anatase phase (JCPDS# 21-1272) of (101), (004), (200), (211), (204) and (107) crystal planes, while those of 27.5° and 54.4° are attributed to (110) and (121) crystal planes of TiO2 rutile phase (JCPDS# 21-1276), respectively. For CQD/TiO2 composite, in addition to the diffraction peaks containing the above anatase and rutile phases of TiO2, the XRD pattern appears a broad peak, concentrated around 24.8°, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of carbon. The spectrum only shows the diffraction peaks of carbon and TiO2, without other impurity peaks, proving that the composite contains only carbon quantum dots and TiO2 nanofibers.

In order to investigate the chemical composition and state of the synthesized CQD/TiO2 nanocomposite, the elements were analyzed using XPS. The total survey spectrum and high-resolution XPS patterns of the CQD/TiO2 composite are shown in Fig. 5 . As Fig. 5(a) shows the total spectrum of CQD/TiO2, the peaks located around 285 eV, 458 eV and 530 eV correspond to the characteristic peaks of C 1 s, Ti 2p and O 1 s, respectively. Except for these three characteristic peaks, there are no peaks of other elements in the spectrum, which is consistent with the results of EDS in Fig. 3. The high-resolution C 1 s peaks of CQD/TiO2 composites are fitted to three components (Fig. 5(b)), which are CC bond at 284.45 eV, CO group at 285.98 eV, and the last peak is 288.39 eV for O-C=O bond [34]. Fig. 5(c) shows the high-resolution Ti 2p pattern with peaks at 458.28 eV and 463.96 eV, corresponding to Ti4+ 2p3/2 and Ti4+ 2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks of TiO2, respectively. As shown in the O 1 s spectrum of the composite in Fig. 5(d), the peaks located at 529.52 eV and 532.07 eV in this orbital correspond to TiO and CO bonds, respectively [35]. Both the C 1 s and O 1 s spectrum indicate the presence of carbon‑oxygen bonds in the CQD/TiO2 composite.

Measuring the optimal working temperature is the first step in the gas sensing test. This is because the response values of metal oxide semiconductors typically change with temperature, and the sensors exhibit optimal performance, including the highest response values and the fastest response/recovery times, at the optimal working temperature. Firstly, gas sensing tests for TiO2 nanofibers were performed in the temperature range of 250–350 °C under natural light. Fig. 6(a) shows the gas sensing response curves of TiO2 nanofibers to 30 ppm formaldehyde at different temperatures, and it can be obtained that the response of TiO2 nanofibers increases with the increase of temperature in the range of 250–350 °C, reaches a maximum at 275 °C, and then decreases with higher temperature. Fig. 6(b) shows the response curve of TiO2 to 10 ppm formaldehyde gas at 275 °C, with a response value of 2.53 and a response time of 65 s. However, the response could not be fully recovered, and only 86 % of the response recovered at 300 s.

Previous studies have shown that titanium dioxide is sensitive to UV light, and UV light irradiation can effectively enhance the sensing performance [36]. Therefore, by irradiating 405 nm, 0.8 mW UV light to the TiO2 sample, it was found that the working temperature of TiO2 dropped from 275 °C to room temperature, which means lower power consumption. The response curve of TiO2 to 10 ppm formaldehyde gas under UV excitation at 25 °C is shown in Fig. 6(c). The response time of TiO2 at room temperature is 135 s, and the recovery time is shortened to 85 s. However, compared with TiO2 under natural light, its response value to 10 ppm formaldehyde decreases by 44 %, and the response could not be fully recovered still. The dynamic response curves of the sensor based on TiO2 for different concentrations of formaldehyde at room temperature under UV irradiation are shown in Fig. 6(d). With the injection of formaldehyde, the resistance of TiO2 decreases, showing an n-type response. The response of TiO2 to formaldehyde is approximately zero below the concentration of 10 ppm, and the response value is 1.41 when the concentration reaches 10 ppm, with TiO2 exhibiting an incomplete recovery of response. Although, by irradiating UV light, the pure TiO2 material achieves a lower working temperature, it is difficult to meet the demand of practical applications due to long response/recovery time, incomplete recovery and low response.

The gas sensing properties of pure TiO2 under both natural light and UV light show that UV can significantly reduce the operating temperature to room temperature. In addition, considering that carbon nanomaterials were commonly used in previous studies to modify metal oxides to improve their gas sensing properties [37], in order to solve the shortcomings of high operating temperature and poor recovery characteristics of pure TiO2 materials under natural light, two auxiliary methods of CQD modification and ultraviolet light excitation were adopted to improve the gas sensing performance of TiO2 in this work. The test after grinding and compositing TiO2 with CQD found that CQD/TiO2 responded to 10 ppm VOCs gas under UV light at 25 °C (Fig. 7(a)), indicating that CQD/TiO2 can work at room temperature under UV excitation, so subsequent gas sensing tests were performed under this condition. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 7(a), the CQD/TiO2 sensor exhibits higher selectivity for formaldehyde with the highest response value of 6.5, which is three times higher than ethanol. There is almost no response for ammonia, and lower response values of 1.14 and 1.47 for acetone and methanol, respectively. Fig. 7(b) shows that the response and recovery times of CQD/TiO2 for 10 ppm formaldehyde at room temperature are reduced to 45 s and 60 s, respectively. The response time is reduced by about 100 s and the response is recovered in a shorter time than TiO2 in Fig. 6(c) under the same environment.

Fig. 7(c) shows the dynamic response curves of the sensor based on CQD/TiO2 for different concentrations of formaldehyde at room temperature under UV irradiation. The sensor resistance decreases with the increase in gas concentration, indicating that the sensor response increases with the increase in formaldehyde concentration. In this process, more formaldehyde molecules are absorbed by the sensing material and more electrons are released to the conduction band with the increase of formaldehyde concentration, resulting in an increasing trend of the sensor response. The responses of the sensor based on CQD/TiO2 for 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10 and 20 ppm formaldehyde are 1.62, 1.73, 2.14, 2.98, 4.01, 6.50 and 12.60, respectively, indicating that the sensor can detect lower concentrations of formaldehyde. Moreover, the response value of the CQD/TiO2 sensor is improved >4.6 times compared to that of the pure TiO2 sensor at the same concentration in Fig. 6(d). The gas sensing properties of the three samples in this work are shown in Table 1 . The comparison reveals that the carbon quantum dot modified material not only improves the response/recovery characteristics of the sensor, but also increases the formaldehyde response value while reducing the detection limit.

In order to determine the flexibility of the composite CQD/TiO2 material, flexibility tests were performed on the device, as shown in Fig. 8 (a-c). Fig. 8(a) schematically represents the changes in resistance of the composite sensor at four different curvature radii (1 cm, 0.8 cm, 0.65 cm and 0.5 cm). As shown in Fig. 8(b), the resistance increases by 7 % as the decrease of curvature radius. The results show that CQD/TiO2 has good mechanical properties and the resistance is almost constant when the radius of curvature becomes smaller. Fig. 8(c) shows the resistance changes of the CQD/TiO2 flexible sensor after 0, 20, 50, 100, 200 and 500 bending/stretching cycles, fluctuating below 1.25 %, which could be negligible. Taken together, the above tests illustrate that the sensor based on CQD/TiO2 in this work has good flexibility performance.

The impact of humidity on flexible gas sensors is a crucial aspect that greatly influences their performance. Hence, it is imperative to evaluate the effect of humidity on gas sensors. It was found that the effect of humidity on gas sensors based on metal oxide and carbon nanomaterials is mainly baseline shift or response change [38]. The response of the composite sensor at room temperature to 10 ppm formaldehyde at different humidity levels was tested, as shown in Fig. 9(a). The rapid decrease in response with increasing humidity may be the result of competition between water molecules and formaldehyde, with a response as low as 1.17 at 62 % humidity and zero at humidity >70 %, indicating that humidity has a large effect on the composite sensor. Additionally, it is worth noting that the baseline resistance of the CQD/TiO2 sensor gradually decreases with the increase in relative humidity. For this phenomenon, most of the current researchers believe that is related to the adsorption of water molecules on the surface of sensitive materials. In higher humidity, water molecules form chemisorbed and physisorbed layers on the material surface, and under the effect of strong electrostatic field in the former, the water molecules in the latter are dissociated into hydronium ions (H3O+) and hydroxyl ions (OH−) as shown in Eq. (1) [39]. The first dissociated H3O+ (1) creates a Grotthuss chain reaction with the next water molecule and subsequently generates the second H3O+ (2) as shown in Eq. (2) [40]. Afterward, as shown in Eq. (3), the hydronium ion hydrates to generate a hydrogen ion (H+) with a higher diffusion coefficient, which undergoes proton hopping between adjacent hydrogen ions, leading to a decrease in the baseline resistance of the sensor [41]. The stability of the sensor is a parameter of considerable importance in commercial applications. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the stability of the CQD/TiO2 sensor was tested for 10 ppm formaldehyde over a period of 30 days (5 days apart). The results show that the average response value to formaldehyde is 6.5 with a fluctuation of 2.6 % over 30 days, which demonstrates good stability.
(1)
2H 2
O
1
→
H 3
O +
1
+
OH −
(2)
H 3
O +
1
+
2H 2

O +
2
+
H 2

(3)
H 3
O +
2
→
H 2
O +
H +

In the end, as shown in Table 2 , the sensing performance of the gas sensor based on CQD/TiO2 prepared in this work was compared with published sensors based on TiO2 or carbon materials. For instance, the working temperature of the sensor prepared in this work is reduced by 150 °C and has a faster response rate compared with Ti3C2/TiO2 [42]. The gas detection limit is reduced by 40 times and the response and recovery times are reduced by 79 % and 91 %, respectively, compared with rGO/ZnO QDs [43]. The carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 nanofiber composites have the advantages of lower working temperature, lower detection limit, and faster response/recovery time, which provide a route to develop high performance formaldehyde detectors at room temperature.

In this work, the gas sensor based on CQD modified TiO2 nanofibers exhibited room temperature operation, high selectivity and fast response/recovery time for formaldehyde, which is mainly attributed to the heterojunction between carbon quantum dots and titanium oxide. It was reported that the work function of n-type TiO2 is 5.13 eV [49] and that of p-type CQD is 5.56 eV [50]. Since TiO2 has a lower work function in comparison, leading to the flow of electrons from TiO2 to CQD and holes from CQD to TiO2. At the same time, the energy band is bent within the electron depletion layer until the Fermi energy level reaches a new equilibrium. When the sensor is exposed to air, oxygen comes into contact with carbon quantum dots on the material surface and generates adsorbed oxygen, such as O−, O2 −, and O2−. As the electron concentration in CQD decreases, the electron depletion layer widens and the channels for electron transfer are obstructed, leading to an increase in the impedance of the composite material, as shown in Fig. 10(a). When the sensor is sent into the formaldehyde environment, the formaldehyde molecules react with the adsorbed oxygen on the surface of the material to generate CO2 and H2O while releasing electrons. As the electrons keep flowing back to the CQD, the electron depletion layer becomes narrower and the impedance of the composite decreases, as shown in Fig. 10(b).

Regarding the mechanism of enhanced gas sensing properties of CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites under UV light irradiation, most of the current research scholars prefer the theory of photogenerated electron transfer between CQD and TiO2 [51]. In the visible light case: only a few electrons can jump due to the wide CQD/TiO2 band gap, as in Fig. 10(c). In the UV case: the absorption energy (hν) of the CQD/TiO2 composite is higher than or equal to the band gap energy, thus generating a large number of conduction band free electrons (e−) and valence band holes (h+) as in Eq. (4), where CQD acts as an electron store, allowing the electrons to be more easily transferred between the CQD and TiO2 surfaces, as shown in Fig. 10(d). These free electrons come into contact with O2 adsorbed on the surface of the material and capture the photogenerated electrons in the conduction band, reducing them to the superoxide radical anion O2 − (Eq. (5)), which then reacts with formaldehyde gas as in Eq. (6). In the humidity environment, besides Eq. (6), the superoxide radical anion O2 − will generate hydro-peroxy radical ·HO2 − with water molecules in the air, and further generates hydroxyl radical ·OH (Eq. (7)), which is considered as the main reactive oxidant for decomposing formaldehyde under UV irradiation in the indoor environment. As shown in Eq. (8), the reaction of ·OH with HCHO can oxidize formaldehyde to form CO2 and H2O.
(4)
CQD /
TiO 2
+ hv →
h +
+
e −
(5)
O 2
+

O 2

(6)

+ HCHO →
CO 2

(7)

O
air
→ ∙
HO 2
−
+ ∙ OH
(8)
2 ∙ OH + HCHO →
CO 2
+
H 2
O +
2H +
+
2e −

In this study, the method of bottom-up approach and electrostatic spinning technique were employed to fabricate the CQD modified TiO2 nanofibers. The SEM, TEM, EDS, XRD and XPS were utilized to examine the morphological structure of the CQD/TiO2 nanocomposites. In order to test the device performance and satisfy the practical requirements of flexibility, gas sensors were fabricated on PET substrates and tested the gas sensing performance at room temperature under UV light of 0.8 mW and 405 nm. The results showed that the working temperature was significantly reduced from 275 °C to room temperature by UV excitation of pure TiO2, and this phenomenon is attributed to photogenerated electron transfer. Additionally, under UV light, the limit of formaldehyde detection by CQD/TiO2 was reduced to 0.5 ppm compared with pure TiO2, while the response and recovery times for 10 ppm formaldehyde were accelerated to 45 and 60 s, respectively. This performance enhancement is attributed to the width variation of the space charge region of the p-n heterojunction formed by CQD and TiO2 in different gas environments. Flexibility testing revealed that the CQD/TiO2 sensor exhibited good sensitivity and excellent flexibility for formaldehyde under UV light at room temperature, and showed good stability. This work provides an experimental reference for the development of wearable room-temperature formaldehyde detectors, but the problem of how to improve the sensitivity in high humidity environment needs to be solved.

Qingyu Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing − original draft. Shuxing Fan: Conceptualization, Data curation, Data interpretation. Lijuan Fu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing − review & editing. Chaoqiang Liu: Investigation. Jiapeng Xu: Investigation. Wei Tang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61801276), and Shandong Province Natural Science Foundation (ZR2018BF003).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Resorcinol-formaldehyde resins as metal-free semiconductor photocatalysts for solar-to-hydrogen peroxide energy conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Shiraishi, T. Takii, T. Hagi, S. Mori, Y. Kofuji, Y. Kitagawa, S. Tanaka, S. Ichikawa, T. Hirai, Resorcinol-formaldehyde resins as metal-free semiconductor photocatalysts for solar-to-hydrogen peroxide energy conversion, Nat. Mater. 18 (9) (2019) 985-993.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of oxidative stress on accommodation and transportation of formaldehyde by lysosome
